title: Corresponding parts of congruent triangles
description: Lets identify corresponding parts of congruent triangles.
topic_path: Topic C: Lessons 17-21: Applications of transformations

assessment_items.question_content:
$\triangle PQR$ is congruent to $\triangle NML$.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c7206117349f09893ed96fdfa1f834297dc1b92d)

**What is the length of  $NM$?**

$\overline{NM} =$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] units

**What is the measure of $\angle MLN$?**

$m\angle MLN = $ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]$\degree$
hints:
There are two ways to solve this problem.

**Way 1:** Pay close attention to the naming of the triangles. The order of the vertices tells you which angles and sides are congruent.

For example, without even looking at the diagram, we know that $\angle \blueD Q$ is congruent to $\angle \blueD M$ because those letters appear second in the names of the triangles: $\triangle P\blueD{Q}R$ and $\triangle N\blueD{M}L$

**Way 2:** Eyeball the triangles to see which angles and sides look the same between the two triangles. But, be careful because this technique might not work when it is hard to see which sides and angles look the same.

In this particular problem, it's pretty clear that the widest angles in each triangle are $\angle \blueD Q$ and $\angle \blueD M$, so we can be pretty sure they're congruent. To be completely sure, we can always go back and try **Way 1**.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c7206117349f09893ed96fdfa1f834297dc1b92d)

Using either approach, we can see that

$\overline{NM} = \overline{PQ} = 9$ units

and

$m\angle MLN = m\angle QRP = 58 \degree$

$\overline{NM} = 9$ units

$m\angle MLN = 58 \degree$

assessment_items.question_content:
$\triangle ABC$ is congruent to $\triangle RQP$.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9a39436f6bd97380d697a2362238669feff1f4a9)

**What is the length of  $PQ$?**

$\overline{PQ} =$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] units

**What is the measure of $\angle PQR$?**

$m\angle PQR = $ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]$\degree$
hints:
There are two ways to solve this problem.

**Way 1:** Pay close attention to the naming of the triangles. The order of the vertices tells you which angles and sides are congruent.

For example, without even looking at the diagram, we know that $\angle \blueD B$ is congruent to $\angle \blueD Q$ because those letters appear second in the names of the triangles: $\triangle A\blueD{B}C$ and $\triangle R\blueD{Q}P$

**Way 2:** Eyeball the triangles to see which angles and sides look the same between the two triangles. But, be careful because this technique might not work when it is hard to see which sides and angles look the same.

In this particular problem, it's pretty clear that the smallest angles in each triangle are $\angle \blueD Q$ and $\angle \blueD B$, so we can be pretty sure they're congruent. To be completely sure, we can always go back and try **Way 1**.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9a39436f6bd97380d697a2362238669feff1f4a9)

Using either approach, we can see that

$\overline{PQ} = \overline{CB} = 9$ units

and

$m\angle PQR = m\angle CBA = 46 \degree$

$\overline{PQ} = 9$ units

$m\angle PQR = 46 \degree$

assessment_items.question_content:
$\triangle ABC$ is congruent to $\triangle ABD$.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/520641f91bb26ca82348cb77273ac83264b346d6)

**What is the length of  $BD$?**

$\overline{BD} =$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] units

**What is the measure of $\angle DAB$?**

$m\angle DAB = $ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]$\degree$
hints:
There are two ways to solve this problem.

**Way 1:** Pay close attention to the naming of the triangles. The order of the vertices tells you which angles and sides are congruent.

For example, without even looking at the diagram, we know that $\angle \blueD C$ is congruent to $\angle \blueD D$ because those letters appear last in the names of the triangles: $\triangle AB\blueD{C}$ and $\triangle AB\blueD{D}$.

**Way 2:** Eyeball the triangles to see which angles and sides look the same between the two triangles. But, be careful because this technique might not work when it is hard to see which sides and angles look the same.

In this particular problem, it's pretty clear that the smallest angles in each triangle are $\angle \blueD C$ and $\angle \blueD D$, so we can be pretty sure they're congruent. To be completely sure, we can always go back and try **Way 1**.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/520641f91bb26ca82348cb77273ac83264b346d6)


Using either approach, we can see that

$\overline{BD} = \overline{BC} = 8$ units

and

$m\angle DAB = m\angle CAB = 53 \degree$

$\overline{BD} = 8$ units

$m\angle DAB = 53 \degree$

assessment_items.question_content:
$\triangle XYZ$ is congruent to $\triangle XWZ$.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4717aaac12bc512c104c4e45eba0ec8281a81a7a)

**What is the length of  $XY$?**

$\overline{XY} =$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] units

**What is the measure of $\angle XYZ$?**

$m\angle XYZ = $ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]$\degree$
hints:
There are two ways to solve this problem.

**Way 1:** Pay close attention to the naming of the triangles. The order of the vertices tells you which angles and sides are congruent.

For example, without even looking at the diagram, we know that $\angle \blueD Y$ is congruent to $\angle \blueD W$ because those letters appear second in the names of the triangles: $\triangle X\blueD{Y}Z$ and $\triangle X\blueD{W}Z$.

**Way 2:** Eyeball the triangles to see which angles and sides look the same between the two triangles. But, be careful because this technique might not work when it is hard to see which sides and angles look the same.

In this particular problem, it's pretty clear that the right angles in each triangle are $\angle \blueD W$ and $\angle \blueD Y$, so we can be pretty sure they're congruent. To be completely sure, we can always go back and try **Way 1**.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4717aaac12bc512c104c4e45eba0ec8281a81a7a)

Using either approach, we can see that

$\overline{XY} = \overline{XW} = 30$ units

and

$m\angle XYZ = m\angle XWZ = 90 \degree$

$\overline{XY} = 30$ units

$m\angle XYZ = 90 \degree$

assessment_items.question_content:
$\triangle MLN$ is congruent to $\triangle YZX$.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8b1671722108cbb0865794ef164c6e45636bfa64)

**What is the length of  $MN$?**

$\overline{MN} =$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] units

**What is the measure of $\angle MLN$?**

$m\angle MLN = $ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]$\degree$
hints:
There are two ways to solve this problem.

**Way 1:** Pay close attention to the naming of the triangles. The order of the vertices tells you which angles and sides are congruent.

For example, without even looking at the diagram, we know that $\angle \blueD L$ is congruent to $\angle \blueD Z$ because those letters appear second in the names of the triangles: $\triangle M\blueD{L}N$ and $\triangle Y\blueD{Z}X$.

**Way 2:** Eyeball the triangles to see which angles and sides look the same between the two triangles. But, be careful because this technique might not work when it is hard to see which sides and angles look the same.

In this particular problem, it's pretty clear that the equal angles in each triangle are $\angle \blueD L$ and $\angle \blueD Z$, so we can be pretty sure they're congruent. To be completely sure, we can always go back and try **Way 1**.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8b1671722108cbb0865794ef164c6e45636bfa64)

Using either approach, we can see that

$\overline{MN} = \overline{YX} = 4$ units

and

$m\angle MLN = m\angle YZX = 42 \degree$

$\overline{MN} =  4$ units

$m\angle MLN =42 \degree$

assessment_items.question_content:
$\triangle ABC$ is congruent to $\triangle BAD$.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/255b53ecfc9622fc614f7944f664cb9be98ba6c9)

**What is the length of  $BD$?**

$\overline{BD} =$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] units

**What is the measure of $\angle BAD$?**

$m\angle BAD = $ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]$\degree$
hints:
There are two ways to solve this problem.

**Way 1:** Pay close attention to the naming of the triangles. The order of the vertices tells you which angles and sides are congruent.

For example, without even looking at the diagram, we know that $\angle \blueD C$ is congruent to $\angle \blueD D$ because those letters appear last in the names of the triangles: $\triangle AB\blueD{C}$ and $\triangle BA\blueD{D}$.

**Way 2:** Eyeball the triangles to see which angles and sides look the same between the two triangles. But, be careful because this technique might not work when it is hard to see which sides and angles look the same.

In this particular problem, it's pretty clear that the angles opposite to the side $AB$ in each triangle are $\angle \blueD C$ and $\angle \blueD D$, so we can be pretty sure they're congruent. To be completely sure, we can always go back and try **Way 1**.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/255b53ecfc9622fc614f7944f664cb9be98ba6c9)


Using either approach, we can see that

$\overline{BD} = \overline{AC} = 16$ units

and

$m\angle BAD = m\angle ABC = 45 \degree$

$\overline{BD} = 16$ units

$m\angle BAD = 45 \degree$

assessment_items.question_content:
$\triangle PQR$ is congruent to $\triangle TSR$.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/96fd0c10cef42bef6b0fb45569cfe9337bf187aa)

**What is the length of  $PR$?**

$\overline{PR} =$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] units

**What is the measure of $\angle PQR$?**

$m\angle PQR = $ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]$\degree$
hints:
There are two ways to solve this problem.

**Way 1:** Pay close attention to the naming of the triangles. The order of the vertices tells you which angles and sides are congruent.

For example, without even looking at the diagram, we know that $\angle \blueD P$ is congruent to $\angle \blueD T$ because those letters appear first in the names of the triangles: $\triangle \blueD{P}QR$ and $\triangle \blueD{T}SR$.

**Way 2:** Eyeball the triangles to see which angles and sides look the same between the two triangles. But, be careful because this technique might not work when it is hard to see which sides and angles look the same.

In this particular problem, it's pretty clear that the smallest angles in each triangle are $\angle \blueD Q$ and $\angle \blueD S$, so we can be pretty sure they're congruent. To be completely sure, we can always go back and try **Way 1**.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/96fd0c10cef42bef6b0fb45569cfe9337bf187aa)

Using either approach, we can see that

$\overline{PR} = \overline{TR} = 11$ units

and

$m\angle PQR = m\angle TSR = 39 \degree$

$\overline{PR} = 11$ units

$m\angle PQR = 39 \degree$

assessment_items.question_content:
The two triangles here have the same shape and size.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5ba017e03a6eb2fe1605e8a02c3002bb016d3ec4)

**Which triangle is $\triangle PQR$ congruent to?**  

*Note: Use the triangle symbol first and make sure the order of the vertices is correct.*

[[☃ sorter 1]]
hints:
We know that $\triangle PQR$ is congruent to the triangle with vertices $L$, $M$, and $N$. 

To find the correct order of these vertices, we can look at some of the corresponding sides and angles.

We can start with any of the three but let's go with the first vertex of $\triangle PQR$ which is  $P$.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5ba017e03a6eb2fe1605e8a02c3002bb016d3ec4)

Vertex $P$ is between two sides whose lengths are $10$ and $9$. 

We see the same thing with vertex $N$. This means that vertex $N$ corresponds to vertex $P$.

Let's try to match the remaining vertices as well.

Similarly, vertex $Q$ sits between two sides whose lengths are $7$ and $9$. We see the same thing with vertex $M$. This means that vertex $Q$ corresponds to vertex $M$.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5ba017e03a6eb2fe1605e8a02c3002bb016d3ec4)


This leaves us with vertex $R$ and $L$ which means that they are corresponding as well.

Putting it all together, we get $\triangle PQR  \cong \triangle NML$.
$\triangle PQR$ is congruent to $\triangle NML$.

assessment_items.question_content:
The two triangles here have the same shape and size.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ebe00e0969c635474d9ee2d98ca8829a489fa860)

**Which triangle is $\triangle QRP$ congruent to?**

*Note: Use the triangle symbol first and make sure the order of the vertices is correct.*

[[☃ sorter 1]]
hints:
We know that $\triangle QRP$ is congruent to the triangle with vertices $A$, $B$, and $C$. 

To find the correct order of these vertices, we can look at some of the corresponding sides and angles.

We can start with any of the three but let's go with the first vertex of $\triangle QRP$ which is  $Q$.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ebe00e0969c635474d9ee2d98ca8829a489fa860)

Measure of $\angle Q$ is $46 \degree$. 

Luckily, measure of $\angle B$ is also $46 \degree$. This means that vertex $Q$ corresponds to vertex $B$.

Let's try to match the remaining vertices as well.

Similarly,  $\angle R$ and $\angle A$ both measure $75 \degree$. This means that vertex $R$ corresponds to vertex $A$.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ebe00e0969c635474d9ee2d98ca8829a489fa860)

This leaves us with vertex $P$ and $C$ which means that they are corresponding as well.

Putting it all together, we get $\triangle QRP  \cong \triangle BAC$.
$\triangle QRP$ is congruent to $\triangle BAC$.

assessment_items.question_content:
The two triangles here are congruent.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/94cd4f3017b52ff0b0cffe3d658c45921578962c)

**Which triangle is $\triangle BAC$ congruent to?**

*Note: Use the triangle symbol first and make sure the order of the vertices is correct.*

[[☃ sorter 1]]
hints:
We know that $\triangle BAC$ is congruent to the triangle with vertices $A$, $B$, and $D$. 

To find the correct order of these vertices, we can look at some of the corresponding sides and angles.

We can start with any of the three but let's go with the first vertex of $\triangle BAC$ which is  $B$.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/94cd4f3017b52ff0b0cffe3d658c45921578962c)

Measure of $\angle ABC$ is $90 \degree$. 

Measure of $\angle ABD$ is also $90 \degree$. This means that vertex $B$ corresponds to itself.

Let's try to match the remaining vertices as well.

Similarly,  $\angle C$ and $\angle D$ both measure $37 \degree$. This means that vertex $C$ corresponds to vertex $D$.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/94cd4f3017b52ff0b0cffe3d658c45921578962c)

This leaves us with vertex $A$, which means that it corresponds to itself as well.

Putting it all together, we get $\triangle BAC  \cong \triangle BAD$.
$\triangle BAC$ is congruent to $\triangle BAD$.

assessment_items.question_content:
The two triangles here are congruent.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9ed1ca8683b77b0620ceab4d0d5073be3b0c6d16)

**Which triangle is $\triangle XZY$ congruent to?**

*Note: Use the triangle symbol first and make sure the order of the vertices is correct.*

[[☃ sorter 1]]
hints:
We know that $\triangle XZY$ is congruent to the triangle with vertices $X$, $Y$, and $W$. 

To find the correct order of these vertices, we can look at some of the corresponding sides and angles.

We can start with any of the three but let's go with the last vertex of $\triangle XZY$ which is  $Y$.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9ed1ca8683b77b0620ceab4d0d5073be3b0c6d16)

Measure of $\angle Y$ is $90 \degree$. 

Luckily, measure of $\angle W$ is also $90 \degree$. This means that vertex $Y$ corresponds to vertex $W$.

Let's try to match the remaining vertices as well.

Similarly,  the angles on vertex $Z$, $\angle WZX$ and $\angle YZX$ both measure $37 \degree$. This means that vertex $Z$ corresponds to itself.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9ed1ca8683b77b0620ceab4d0d5073be3b0c6d16)

This leaves us with vertex $X$, which means that it corresponds to itself as well.

Putting it all together, we get $\triangle XZY  \cong \triangle XZW$.
$\triangle XZY$ is congruent to $\triangle XZW$.

assessment_items.question_content:
The two triangles here have the same shape and size.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/53657b597728c26e369d0631f002ce87af98c77a)

**Which triangle is $\triangle NLM$ congruent to?**

*Note: Use the triangle symbol first and make sure the order of the vertices is correct.*

[[☃ sorter 1]]
hints:
We know that $\triangle NLM$ is congruent to the triangle with vertices $X$, $Y$, and $Z$. 

To find the correct order of these vertices, we can look at some of the corresponding sides and angles.

We can start with any of the three but let's go with the first vertex of $\triangle NLM$ which is  $N$.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/53657b597728c26e369d0631f002ce87af98c77a)

Measure of $\angle N$ is $22 \degree$. 

Luckily, measure of $\angle X$ is also $22 \degree$. This means that vertex $N$ corresponds to vertex $X$.

Let's try to match the remaining vertices as well.

Similarly,  $\angle L$ and $\angle Z$ both measure $42 \degree$. This means that vertex $L$ corresponds to vertex $Z$.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/53657b597728c26e369d0631f002ce87af98c77a)

This leaves us with vertex $M$ and $Y$ which means that they are corresponding as well.

Putting it all together, we get $\triangle NLM  \cong \triangle XZY$.
$\triangle NLM$ is congruent to $\triangle XZY$.

assessment_items.question_content:
The two triangles here have the same shape and size.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3ffb0e1812b670ee5d3ca48cb757034ef95a0852)

**Which triangle is $\triangle BAD$ congruent to?**

*Note: Use the triangle symbol first and make sure the order of the vertices is correct.*

[[☃ sorter 1]]
hints:
We know that $\triangle BAD$ is congruent to the triangle with vertices $A$, $B$, and $C$. 

To find the correct order of these vertices, we can look at some of the corresponding sides and angles.

We can start with any of the three but let's go with the first vertex of $\triangle BAD$ which is  $B$.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3ffb0e1812b670ee5d3ca48cb757034ef95a0852)

Vertex $B$ is between two sides whose lengths are $16$ and $20$. 

We see the same thing with vertex $A$. This means that vertex $B$ corresponds to vertex $A$.

Let's try to match the remaining vertices as well.

Similarly,  $\angle D$ and $\angle C$ both measure $59 \degree$. This means that vertex $D$ corresponds to vertex $C$.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3ffb0e1812b670ee5d3ca48cb757034ef95a0852)

This leaves us with vertex $A$ and $B$ which means that they are corresponding as well.

Putting it all together, we get $\triangle BAD  \cong \triangle ABC$.
$\triangle BAD$ is congruent to $\triangle ABC$.

assessment_items.question_content:
The two triangles here have the same shape and size.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e269366736c330b4d9ec793a7d44592839791cf2)

**Which triangle is $\triangle RQP$ congruent to?**

*Note: Use the triangle symbol first and make sure the order of the vertices is correct.*

[[☃ sorter 1]]
hints:
We know that $\triangle RQP$ is congruent to the triangle with vertices $R$, $S$, and $T$. 

To find the correct order of these vertices, we can look at some of the corresponding sides and angles.

We can start with any of the three but let's go with the middle vertex of $\triangle RQP$ which is  $Q$.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e269366736c330b4d9ec793a7d44592839791cf2)

Vertex $Q$ is between two sides whose lengths are $18$ and $13$. 

We see the same thing with vertex $S$. This means that vertex $Q$ corresponds to vertex $S$.

Let's try to match the remaining vertices as well.

Similarly,  $\angle P$ and $\angle T$ both measure $45 \degree$. This means that vertex $P$ corresponds to vertex $T$.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e269366736c330b4d9ec793a7d44592839791cf2)

This leaves us with vertex $R$ which means it corresponds to itself.

Putting it all together, we get $\triangle RQP  \cong \triangle RST$.
$\triangle RQP$ is congruent to $\triangle RST$.
